speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Before we get into Git commands, we need the picture in our heads that makes those commands make sense. Today is all about that mental model: what Git is storing, where your changes live, what a commit really is, what a branch really is, how your computer relates to GitHub, and why conflicts happen.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that framing, because a lot of learners can follow a recipe right up until something unexpected happens. Then suddenly every word in the terminal feels mysterious.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And this lesson is designed to prevent that. On GitHub's website, you may have edited files without thinking about Git underneath. The site handled the commit creation, branch management, and history tracking for you. Locally, you do those operations yourself. The commands are learnable, but the real goal is understanding what each one changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not really a memorization lesson. It's a how-to-think lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. And one practical note: later Git practice depends on this model. If you understand the three areas, branches, and the difference between local and remote, Day 2 Git work becomes much more predictable. There's also a learning principle here that matters for accessibility and confidence: first understand local Git mechanics, then connect them to GitHub workflows and tools like the GitHub CLI. That order makes terminal work easier to recover from.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants deeper documentation after this episode?

speaker: ALEX
text: The core ideas here line up with the Pro Git book, Git SCM documentation, GitHub Docs, and the GitHub Accessibility Lab CLI guide. There are also related appendices on advanced Git, authentication, and Git security for later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the most important concept in this whole lesson: Git has three main areas.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the part people hear about, but often only vaguely. Working tree, staging, repository, something like that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and we want it to be concrete. First, the working directory. That's the folder on your computer where the files actually live. If you open a project in VS Code and browse files in the Explorer, you're looking at the working directory. These are ordinary files on disk. You can edit them, rename them, delete them, create new ones.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I type in a Markdown file and save it, I've changed the working directory.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Git notices that change, but Git does not automatically preserve it in history. Noticing is not the same as recording.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's where the staging area comes in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The staging area, also called the index, is a holding zone. When you decide a particular change belongs in your next save point, you stage it. You're telling Git, include this in the next commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So staging is like selecting what counts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect phrasing. You can stage one file and leave another unstaged. You can group related changes together. That's one of Git's big strengths: your commit can reflect a meaningful unit of work instead of every edit you've made that afternoon.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then the repository is the permanent record.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The repository is where Git stores committed history. Inside your project is a hidden folder named dot git, written as.git. That's where Git keeps its metadata and history. When you commit, Git takes the staged content and stores it in the repository as a snapshot.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's connect the flow clearly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always: edit in the working directory, stage the changes you want, then commit the staged changes into the repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Edit, stage, commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In command terms, that often looks like: edit a file, run git add on the file, then run git commit with a message. But focus on the idea, not just the syntax.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I want to call out something accessibility-related from the source material. A lot of visual Git tutorials use arrows and diagrams. But the actual information is just that three-step flow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You do not need a diagram to understand it. The text sequence is the same content. Edit in the working directory. Stage into the index. Commit into the repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we use the packing analogy?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Imagine your desk is covered with papers and objects. That's the working directory. An open box on the floor is the staging area. You choose what goes into that box. When you're satisfied, you seal the box, label it, and put it on the shelf. That's the commit. The sealed, stored box is now part of the permanent record.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And before sealing it, I can add things or remove things from the box.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can stage and unstage as much as needed before committing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make this practical for different learners in VS Code and the terminal.

speaker: ALEX
text: For screen reader users, git status is extremely helpful. Run it in the terminal and Git tells you which files are changed, which are staged, and which are untracked. In VS Code Source Control, usually opened with Control Shift G, files are grouped into Changes and Staged Changes, and you can move through them with arrow keys. Press Enter on a file to hear the diff, with added and removed lines announced.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for low vision learners?

speaker: ALEX
text: In Source Control, staged files appear in a separate Staged Changes group, often with a green plus icon. Unstaged files appear under Changes, often with an orange modified indicator. If the panel is hard to read, increase zoom or font size. In the editor gutter, added lines and modified lines are also marked by color.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted users, the Source Control badge count and the file status letters like M, U, A, and D give a fast visual summary.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's talk about commits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: People often say a commit is a save point. That's helpful, but a little incomplete.

speaker: ALEX
text: Agreed. A commit is a snapshot of your project at a moment in time. Importantly, it's not just a loose note saying what changed. Git records the state of the staged files as they are at commit time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a commit contains more than a message.

speaker: ALEX
text: Much more. Four key parts matter here. First, the snapshot: the recorded state of the staged files. Second, the message: a human-readable description, like fix broken link in setup guide. Third, the author information: who made the commit, typically name and email. Fourth, a parent pointer: a reference to the commit that came before it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That parent pointer is what turns separate save points into a timeline.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Each commit knows what came before, so Git can follow history backward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And every commit gets an ID.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A unique identifier called a hash, specifically a SHA hash. The full form is a 40-character string. In practice, you'll often see just the first seven characters because that short form is usually enough to identify the commit in a given repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So learners do not need to memorize long strings of random-looking characters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Not at all. You usually copy, tab-complete, or use the short form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about permanence? People hear that Git never forgets, then later hear about rewriting history and get confused.

speaker: ALEX
text: A good way to phrase it is: commits are permanent, mostly. Once a commit is part of the repository history, it exists in that timeline. You can create later commits that reverse its effects, but the original commit is still part of history. That stability is one reason Git feels safe. You can usually get back to earlier states. There are advanced history-rewriting tools, but for this mental model, think of commits as durable recorded points.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this connects directly back to Day 1 on GitHub.com.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. When you clicked Commit changes in the web editor, GitHub handled staging and committing in one web action. Locally, those steps are separated. That separation gives you more control.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Accessibility tips here too?

speaker: ALEX
text: For screen reader users, after committing, git log dash dash oneline dash 5 is a great command to hear the last five commits as short hashes and messages. In VS Code Source Control, move to the commit message box, type your message, then use the commit shortcut, often Control Enter. And remember, the short hash is enough for most commands.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For low vision users, widening the Source Control panel can make the message field easier to read, and after a successful commit the Source Control badge count usually drops to zero.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And the Timeline view in VS Code can also help learners inspect file history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next up: branches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is another word people use constantly, and beginners sometimes imagine branches as separate folders or duplicate repositories.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a very common misconception. A branch is best understood as a movable pointer to a commit. It marks the tip of a line of development. When you make a new commit on that branch, the branch label moves forward to point to the new commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the branch name is not the work itself. It's a label pointing to the latest commit in that line.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And the default branch in many repositories is called main. That's usually the primary line of development.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What happens when I create a new branch?

speaker: ALEX
text: Git creates a new pointer that starts at your current commit. At first, the new branch and the old branch point to the same place. Then, once you make commits on the new branch, that branch moves forward while the other stays where it was.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So branches let timelines diverge without interfering with each other.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely. That's why they're so useful for features, fixes, experiments, and pull request workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's define HEAD, because learners see it everywhere.

speaker: ALEX
text: HEAD tells you what you're currently on. More precisely, it identifies the current checked-out branch or commit. In everyday use, think of HEAD as Git's answer to the question, where am I right now? If HEAD is on your feature branch, new commits go there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when you switch branches, your working directory updates to reflect that branch's files, as much as Git can safely do.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we turn that into a simple mental card?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Main is the usual default branch. Creating a branch does not duplicate the whole project in a clumsy way; it creates a new pointer from the current commit. Commits made while HEAD is on that branch advance that branch. HEAD tells you your current position.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in tools, learners can often check the current branch in the VS Code status bar or with git branch or git status in the terminal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's widen the picture from one computer to two locations: local and remote.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is where Git and GitHub get tangled in people's minds.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Git is the version control system on your machine. GitHub is a hosting service for remote repositories. Your local repository is the Git history stored on your computer, inside that hidden.git folder in the project. The remote repository is a copy hosted somewhere else, often on GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when people say, I committed my work, that might only mean locally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A local commit records history on your computer. It does not automatically publish anything to GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is such an important distinction.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really is. Think in terms of a two-copy model. You usually have one repository copy on your computer and another on the remote host. They are related, but they are not magically identical every second. You must intentionally sync them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And sync goes in both directions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You push local commits to share them with the remote. You fetch or pull remote changes to bring them to your local environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So local is where I work and experiment. Remote is where I share and collaborate.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a solid basic model. Also, the remote is especially important in open source because it becomes the shared reference point for collaborators, issues, pull requests, code review, and CI workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any learning card version of this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Local repository: on your computer. Remote repository: hosted elsewhere, often GitHub. They start related but can drift apart. Syncing them requires explicit operations. And a commit is not the same thing as a push.

speaker: ALEX
text: That brings us directly to push, pull, and fetch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: These three get mashed together in beginners' minds.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's separate them clearly. Push means send your local commits to the remote repository. It's how you share work you've already committed locally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I edit a file and push without committing, nothing meaningful happens because the change never became a local commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Push shares commits, not loose unstaged edits in your working directory.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about pull?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pull means get changes from the remote and integrate them into your local branch. In everyday usage, pull is often thought of as fetch plus merge, or fetch plus rebase depending on configuration, but for this mental model the key point is that pull brings remote work down and combines it with your local state.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And fetch is the quieter one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Fetch checks the remote for updates and downloads information about new commits, but it does not merge them into your current branch. It's a safe way to see what's changed before integrating anything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I want awareness without immediate change to my current branch, fetch is the cautious choice.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Push shares your work. Pull gets others' work and updates your branch. Fetch gets the information first without merging.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That distinction is great for learners who want to stay oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, one of the most emotionally charged Git topics: merge conflicts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because people often read the word conflict as failure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, but conflicts are normal. A merge conflict usually happens when Git cannot automatically decide how to combine competing changes. The classic case is two different edits to the same part of the same file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I changed line 20 one way and someone else changed line 20 another way, Git may need a human decision.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Git is very good at auto-merging non-overlapping changes. But when the same region was changed in incompatible ways, Git stops and asks you to resolve it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does that look like?

speaker: ALEX
text: In a conflicted file, Git inserts conflict markers that show the competing versions. One section represents your current branch's content, another represents the incoming change, and markers divide them. Your job is to edit the file into the final correct version, removing the markers and choosing or combining the content as needed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal is not to pick a side automatically. The goal is to produce the right final text.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Sometimes you keep your version. Sometimes the other version. Sometimes a blend of both. After editing the file so it's correct, you stage the resolved file and complete the merge process with a commit if needed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the important emotional lesson is that conflicts are not evidence you've broken Git.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. They are a normal consequence of collaborative editing. In fact, a conflict is Git being careful. Instead of guessing and possibly damaging content, Git pauses for your decision.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any accessibility note here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so. Screen reader users may find conflict markers easier to understand in a plain text editor or VS Code diff view because the markers are literal text in the file. Listening carefully to marker boundaries and surrounding lines can make the structure clear. Low vision and sighted users may benefit from VS Code's merge editor, but the underlying plain-text markers are the essential concept for everyone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the learning card version concise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Conflicts happen when Git cannot safely combine changes by itself. A conflicted file contains markers showing the competing edits. You resolve by editing the file into the correct final form, removing markers, then staging the resolution. And conflicts are normal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's zoom out to the Git timeline.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is where the parent pointers and branches start to feel like a map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Because each commit points back to its parent, Git history forms a timeline. Reading the timeline means understanding that commits are connected in sequence. If you look at a log, you're seeing points in that history, usually newest first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And branching creates parallel timelines.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If you create a branch and commit on it, you now have a line of development that moves separately from main. Both lines share earlier history, then diverge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And merging reconnects those timelines.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. When you merge, Git creates a commit that brings the lines back together, preserving the fact that parallel work existed. So the history is not just one straight line forever. It can branch and reconnect.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That helps explain why Git feels more like a graph than a stack of files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly, though for beginners it's often enough to picture a timeline with occasional side paths that later rejoin.

speaker: ALEX
text: So let's put the whole model together.

speaker: JAMIE
text: A checklist would help.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here's a compact one. Your files live in the working directory. You choose what belongs in the next snapshot by staging it. A commit records the staged state in the repository with a message, author, and link to previous history. A branch is a movable pointer to the latest commit in one line of work. HEAD tells you your current position. Your local repository on your computer and the remote repository on GitHub are separate copies that must be synced intentionally. Push sends your commits up. Pull brings remote changes down and integrates them. Fetch checks for updates without merging. Conflicts happen when Git needs human help combining overlapping changes. And the overall history is a timeline that can branch and merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of checklist a learner can rehearse before typing commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If a command seems confusing, ask: which area am I changing, which copy am I changing, and which branch am I on?

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great recovery framework.

speaker: ALEX
text: And if you do get stuck, here's the reassuring part. Most Git confusion becomes manageable when you slow down and inspect your state.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning, don't panic and start pasting random commands from the internet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Please don't. Instead, check where you are and what Git thinks is happening. Commands like git status are often the best first step. Look at the current branch. Look at which files are changed, staged, or untracked. Check whether your commits are only local or also pushed. Think in terms of the mental model you built today.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if the issue involves authentication, advanced history editing, or security concerns, that's where those related appendices come in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use the right source for the right problem. For trusted references, lean on Git SCM docs, GitHub Docs, the Pro Git book, GitHub flow guidance, pull request documentation, and accessibility-focused CLI guidance from GitHub's Accessibility Lab.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the big takeaway is not just Git stores history. It's that Git has a structure: areas, snapshots, pointers, copies, and sync actions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Once you understand that structure, Git stops feeling like magic and starts feeling like a system you can reason about.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that's when commands become less scary, because they each have a place in the model.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. In the next practice-oriented work, you'll use this model directly while cloning, staging, committing, branching, and syncing with GitHub. Keep the mental picture active while you work, and every command will have a why behind it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's it for this lesson. Take a breath, maybe repeat the flow out loud: edit, stage, commit; local and remote; push, pull, fetch.

speaker: ALEX
text: And remember: confusion usually means you need to inspect state, not that you've failed. We'll see you next time.
